title: Design Patterns Overview: How to Learn, Apply, and Combine Them
excerpt: A practical overview of design patterns: what they are, where they came from, when to use them, and how to build real mastery instead of memorizing names.

body.value:
A design pattern is a general, reusable solution to a recurring software design problem in a specific context.

It is not a finished library, framework, or copy-paste implementation. It is a proven structure for solving a kind of problem that appears again and again.

Patterns matter because they help developers move from accidental design to intentional design. Instead of inventing a brand-new approach every time, you can recognize the situation and apply a known solution.

The idea of patterns did not begin in software. It came from architecture and urban design, where Christopher Alexander described recurring solutions for towns, buildings, and spaces.

Software developers later adapted this idea to object-oriented design. The most influential milestone was the classic Gang of Four book, which organized software design patterns into categories and gave the industry a shared vocabulary.

That shared vocabulary is one of the biggest reasons patterns became so valuable. Teams could stop describing every solution from scratch and start talking at the level of intent.

value.caption: How the idea of patterns moved into software
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Architecture[City and building architecture] --> RecurringSolutions[Recurring solutions to recurring problems]
RecurringSolutions --> Software[Software design]
Software --> PatternCatalog[Named pattern catalogs]
PatternCatalog --> SharedLanguage[Shared design language for teams]

body.value:
Patterns help you avoid reinventing the wheel. Many design problems have already been explored, discussed, refined, and tested in real systems. Knowing this saves time and reduces accidental complexity.

Patterns also improve communication. Saying use a repository or this should be a strategy is much clearer than describing a long sequence of refactorings in a code review.

Most importantly, patterns help you produce software that is easier to change. They often reduce coupling, isolate variation, and make behavior easier to extend, test, and reason about.

value.caption: Why patterns improve development
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
RecurringProblem --> RecognizePattern
RecognizePattern --> ReuseKnownStructure
ReuseKnownStructure --> BetterCommunication
ReuseKnownStructure --> BetterDesign
BetterDesign --> EasierTesting
BetterDesign --> EasierChange
BetterCommunication --> FasterTeamAlignment

body.value:
One of the most practical benefits of patterns is communication. Without a pattern name, a developer might explain a solution as a series of low-level refactorings: extract an interface, move persistence logic, inject it into the class, and stop calling the database directly.

With pattern vocabulary, the same idea becomes much simpler: use the Repository pattern to decouple data access.

That shift is important. It compresses design discussion into a shared concept and lets the team focus on trade-offs instead of mechanics.

value.title: How to learn patterns the right way

body.value:
Learning patterns is not about memorizing a catalog. Real learning happens in stages.

First, there is ignorance: you do not know the pattern exists. Then comes awareness: you have heard of it and know it might help. After that comes experimentation: you try it, often too eagerly, and begin discovering where it fits and where it does not. Finally comes mastery: you can recognize opportunities quickly and apply the pattern with confidence and restraint.

The best goal is to become T-shaped. Know many patterns broadly enough to follow design conversations, but go deep on the ones that appear often in the systems you actually build.

value.caption: Practical learning path for design patterns
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Ignorance[Ignorance] --> Awareness[Awareness]
Awareness --> Experimentation[Experimentation]
Experimentation --> Mastery[Mastery]
Mastery --> Judgment[Good judgment about fit and trade-offs]

body.value:
A mature pattern is more than a name. It usually includes a standard set of information: its name, classification, intent, motivation, applicability, structure, participants, collaborations, consequences, implementation guidance, known uses, and related patterns.

If you only want broad awareness, focus first on three things: the name, the intent, and the situations where the pattern applies. That is enough to recognize when a pattern might help.

If you want mastery, you must go deeper. You should understand the structure, the participant roles, the collaboration between them, and the trade-offs the pattern introduces.

value.caption: What makes up a design pattern
value.diagram:
mindmap
  root((Design Pattern))
    Name
    Intent
    Motivation
    Applicability
    Structure
    Participants
    Collaboration
    Consequences
    Implementation
    Known Uses
    Related Patterns

value.title: How diagrams fit into pattern thinking

body.value:
Patterns are often taught with UML-style structure diagrams because patterns are fundamentally about relationships. A diagram helps you see abstractions, implementations, dependencies, and interactions at a glance.

But structure alone is not enough. Several patterns can look similar in diagrams while serving different purposes. Two patterns may share almost the same shape yet differ completely in intent.

That is why pattern learning should always connect structure with context. Do not just memorize diagrams. Learn what problem the structure is solving.

value.caption: The same abstraction can support multiple implementations
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Client --> IService
IService --> RealService
IService --> Proxy
Proxy --> RealService

body.value:
The worst time to apply a newly learned pattern is impulsively in production code. New pattern knowledge often leads to overuse. Everything starts to look like a nail.

A better approach is to practice in a controlled environment: coding katas, refactoring exercises, and small experiments backed by tests. That lets you understand the pattern without risking critical code.

When applying patterns to real code, use refactoring discipline. Start with test coverage, make changes in small steps, work in a separate branch, review the result, and confirm behavior is unchanged. If the pattern does not fit, delete the attempt and keep the lesson.

value.caption: Safe pattern adoption workflow
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
LearnPattern --> PracticeInKata
PracticeInKata --> AddTests
AddTests --> RefactorInSmallSteps
RefactorInSmallSteps --> ReviewDesign
ReviewDesign --> KeepOrDiscard
KeepOrDiscard --> BetterJudgment

value.title: Patterns become more powerful when combined

body.value:
In real systems, patterns are rarely used alone. Strong designs often come from combining a few simple patterns.

For example, a Strategy can encapsulate one family of behavior, a Repository can isolate persistence, a Decorator can add caching or logging, and a Builder can create a configured object safely. Each pattern handles a different kind of responsibility.

This matters because patterns are not trophies. Their value is not in how many you know, but in how well you combine them to solve a real problem with minimal complexity.

value.caption: Pattern combinations in a realistic design
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Client --> Service
Service --> Strategy
Service --> Repository
Repository --> Decorator
Decorator --> DataStore
Builder --> Service

body.value: This example shows how a pattern name compresses a design decision into something concrete. The service depends on an abstraction instead of directly calling persistence logic. That is the core benefit behind a Repository-style design.

value.caption: A simple repository-based design in C#

body.value:
If you want a practical starting point, begin with patterns that appear often in application code.

  • Strategy helps when behavior varies and large conditionals start growing.
  • Repository helps isolate persistence concerns from business logic.
  • Adapter helps when two interfaces do not naturally fit together.
  • Factory helps when object creation is complex or should be hidden.
  • Proxy and Decorator help add control or extra behavior around an object.
  • Singleton is worth understanding, both for how it works and why it is often misused.

You do not need to master every pattern at once. Start with the ones that solve problems you see every week.

• Memorizing names without understanding intent
  • Applying a pattern before a real design problem exists
  • Choosing patterns because they look clever rather than because they fit
  • Ignoring trade-offs such as added indirection and complexity
  • Using production code as a first practice environment
  • Learning structures but not learning the context where they help
  • Treating one favorite pattern as a universal solution

value.title: Design patterns are design tools
value.text: Patterns help you recognize recurring problems, communicate intent clearly, and apply proven structures instead of inventing everything from scratch. Learn them broadly, practice them deliberately, and use them only when the problem truly matches.

title: C# Strategy Pattern Explained: Runtime Behavior Without Conditionals Everywhere
excerpt: Learn how the Strategy pattern works in C#, why it improves extensibility and testability, and how to apply it for tax calculation, invoice delivery, shipping providers, and existing .NET APIs like Array.Sort.

body.value:
The Strategy pattern is one of the most practical design patterns in everyday C# development. It helps when an application needs to perform the same kind of operation in multiple different ways, and the exact behavior should be selected at runtime.

Instead of placing all variations inside one growing class filled with if, switch, or nested conditionals, Strategy moves each algorithm into its own implementation behind a shared interface.

The result is a design where behavior becomes replaceable. The object using the behavior does not need to know the implementation details. It only needs to know the contract.

value.caption: Core idea of the Strategy pattern
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Context --> IStrategy
IStrategy --> StrategyA[ConcreteStrategyA]
IStrategy --> StrategyB[ConcreteStrategyB]
IStrategy --> StrategyC[ConcreteStrategyC]

body.value:
The Strategy pattern is usually described through three roles.

  • Context: the object that needs some behavior but should not own all implementation details.
  • Strategy: an interface that defines the operation the context can use.
  • Concrete Strategy: a specific implementation of that operation.

In business code, the context is often something like an order service, checkout workflow, pricing engine, or export processor. The strategy interface captures the behavior that varies, and the concrete strategies implement the actual rules.

value.title: A good rule of thumb
value.text: When the same business action can happen in several valid ways and the choice should be made at runtime, Strategy is often a strong candidate.

body.value: This is the minimal shape of a Strategy pattern in C#. The Order is the context. It delegates tax calculation to whatever implementation of ISalesTaxStrategy it receives.

value.caption: Minimal Strategy pattern in C#

value.title: Why this is better than a large conditional

body.value:
Without Strategy, the context often accumulates country checks, delivery type checks, provider checks, and API-specific logic. That produces code that is harder to maintain, harder to test, and more dangerous to change.

With Strategy, each variation lives in a focused class. Adding a new behavior usually means adding a new implementation instead of editing a giant conditional that already handles many unrelated cases.

value.caption: From conditional-heavy design to replaceable behavior
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
Before[Large context class] --> IfElse[if/switch on country or option]
IfElse --> MixedLogic[Mixed rules and implementation details]

After[Context] --> StrategyInterface[IStrategy]
StrategyInterface --> FocusedA[Focused Strategy A]
StrategyInterface --> FocusedB[Focused Strategy B]
StrategyInterface --> FocusedC[Focused Strategy C]

value.title: Sales tax example: a strong real-world fit

body.value:
A tax calculator is a natural example for Strategy because tax rules vary by country, region, and sometimes item type. The context should not need to know the implementation details for every possible region.

One strategy can calculate Swedish tax rules, another can calculate United States tax rules, and both remain isolated from each other. This keeps the design open for extension without forcing the central order logic to keep growing.

value.caption: A more realistic Swedish tax strategy

body.value:
This is where Strategy becomes especially valuable. You can improve one country-specific implementation without risking accidental changes to the others.

That isolation also makes tests much more focused. A change to Swedish tax rules should not break tests for the United States tax implementation.

value.title: Runtime selection is the key benefit

body.value:
The real power of Strategy is not just extracting classes. It is selecting behavior at runtime based on user input, configuration, or business rules.

For example, if the destination country is Sweden, the application can choose SwedenSalesTaxStrategy. If the destination is the United States, it can choose UnitedStatesSalesTaxStrategy. The context stays unchanged.

value.caption: Runtime decision flow
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
UserInput[User input / order data] --> Resolver[Strategy selection]
Resolver --> Sweden[SwedenSalesTaxStrategy]
Resolver --> USA[UnitedStatesSalesTaxStrategy]
Resolver --> Other[Other region strategy]
Sweden --> Context
USA --> Context
Other --> Context

value.title: An alternative approach: pass the strategy into the method

body.value:
The strategy does not have to be stored as a property on the context. Another valid approach is to pass the strategy directly into the method that needs it.

This is useful when the behavior is more temporary, when you want a one-off override, or when you want to keep the context itself lighter.

value.caption: Passing the strategy directly to the method

body.value: This is still Strategy. The important idea is not where the interface is stored. The important idea is that behavior is represented as an interchangeable implementation behind a contract.

value.title: Invoice delivery is another excellent Strategy example

body.value:
Invoice generation often needs multiple delivery mechanisms: email, file export, print-on-demand, database storage, or perhaps an external billing API.

These are clearly different implementations of the same business capability: generate or deliver an invoice. That makes them a strong fit for Strategy.

value.caption: Invoice delivery strategies
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Order --> IInvoiceStrategy
IInvoiceStrategy --> Email[EmailInvoiceStrategy]
IInvoiceStrategy --> File[FileInvoiceStrategy]
IInvoiceStrategy --> Print[PrintOnDemandInvoiceStrategy]

value.caption: Invoice Strategy with shared base functionality

value.title: Shipping providers show Strategy beyond pure computation

body.value:
Strategy is not limited to calculations. It also works when different providers, services, or APIs must be called in different ways.

A shipping workflow is a perfect example. DHL, FedEx, UPS, USPS, and local postal services may all require different request shapes, validation rules, authentication, and response handling. The business intent is the same, but the algorithm and integration details vary.

value.caption: Shipping providers as strategies

body.value: This keeps each provider-specific integration local to one class. The order or order service simply asks for shipping to happen. It does not need to know how a specific provider works.

value.title: The context can be a service, not only a domain entity

body.value:
In small examples, the context is often modeled as the domain object itself, such as Order. In larger applications, it is often cleaner for the context to be a service layer object like OrderService, CheckoutService, or InvoiceService.

That approach keeps the domain model focused on business state while orchestration and dependency coordination live in the service layer.

value.caption: Service-oriented version of the Strategy pattern
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Controller --> OrderService
OrderService --> TaxStrategy
OrderService --> InvoiceStrategy
OrderService --> ShippingStrategy
OrderService --> Order

body.value:
One of the strongest practical benefits of Strategy is testability.

Each concrete strategy can be tested independently with focused input. At the same time, the context can be tested using mocked or fake strategies to verify orchestration without executing real email, filesystem, tax, or HTTP logic.

value.caption: Testing a context with a fake strategy

value.title: Why testability improves
value.text: The context no longer owns the implementation details. That lets you test the context and each algorithm separately instead of trying to verify one giant class that does everything.

body.value:
The Strategy pattern appears all over .NET, often without explicitly using the word Strategy. A good example is Array.Sort with IComparer<T>.

When you pass a comparer, you are effectively injecting the strategy for how two elements should be compared. The sorting algorithm stays the same, but the comparison behavior can change.

value.caption: Array.Sort with IComparer<T> as a strategy

body.value: This is an important lesson: if a method accepts an interface or abstraction that changes behavior, there is a good chance Strategy is already in play.

• Extensibility: new behaviors can be added by creating new strategies.
  • Cleaner separation of concerns: the context coordinates work, while each strategy owns one algorithm.
  • Reduced conditional complexity: large branching logic is replaced with focused implementations.
  • Testability: strategies and contexts can be tested independently.
  • Runtime flexibility: behavior can be selected based on user input or configuration.

Like every pattern, Strategy improves some things and costs some things.

  • It introduces more classes and abstractions.
  • It can be overkill if there are only one or two trivial variations.
  • Bad naming can make the design harder to understand.
  • If strategy selection logic becomes messy, the creation side may need its own refactoring, often with a factory or resolver.

Use it where variability is real and likely to grow, not just because an interface can be introduced.

value.title: A common mistake
value.text: Do not apply Strategy only to look architecturally sophisticated. Apply it when behavior truly varies and you need to swap algorithms cleanly.

value.title: When Strategy is a strong fit

body.value:
• You have multiple ways to perform the same operation.
  • The choice should happen at runtime.
  • Conditionals are starting to spread through the codebase.
  • Different rules should evolve independently.
  • You want to isolate integrations with different external providers.
  • You want easier unit testing with mocks, stubs, or fake implementations.

value.title: Strategy in one sentence
value.text: Define a family of interchangeable behaviors behind a shared interface, then choose the appropriate implementation at runtime without changing the context.

title: Command Pattern in C#: Decoupling Actions and Enabling Undo/Redo
excerpt: Learn how the Command Pattern works in C#, how to implement it, and why it enables clean architecture, undo/redo functionality, and extensibility in modern applications.

body.value:
The Command Pattern is a behavioral design pattern that turns a request into an object. This allows you to parameterize clients with different requests, queue or log operations, and support undo/redo functionality.

Instead of calling methods directly, you wrap the action inside a command object. This decouples the sender of a request from the receiver.

value.caption: Command Pattern Structure
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Client --> Invoker
Invoker --> Command
Command --> Receiver

body.value:
The Command Pattern consists of four key elements:

  • Command – Encapsulates the request and required data
  • Receiver – Performs the actual work
  • Invoker – Executes the command
  • Client – Creates and configures the command

value.caption: Real-world Example: Shopping Cart
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Button(Client) --> Manager(Invoker)
Manager --> AddToCartCommand(Command)
AddToCartCommand --> ShoppingCart(Receiver)

body.value:
The Command Pattern is powerful because it enables:

  • Decoupling between UI and business logic
  • Undo/Redo functionality
  • Command history tracking
  • Delayed execution
  • Replaying operations

value.text: Commands can be stored and replayed later, making this pattern ideal for event sourcing and recovery scenarios.

body.value: This interface defines the contract for all commands. Each command must know how to execute itself and optionally undo its action.
The CommandManager acts as the invoker. It executes commands and keeps track of them using a stack, enabling undo operations in reverse order.

value.caption: Undo Mechanism (LIFO)
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
Cmd1 --> Cmd2 --> Cmd3
Undo --> Cmd3 --> Cmd2 --> Cmd1

body.value: This command contains everything it needs to execute: references to repositories and the product itself. It also defines how to undo the action.
Instead of calling repositories directly, the application now works through commands:

value.code:
var command = new AddToCartCommand(cartRepo, productRepo, product);
manager.Invoke(command);

body.value: This removes tight coupling between the UI and the data layer.
In WPF, the Command Pattern is built into the framework via ICommand. UI elements like buttons bind directly to commands.
The RelayCommand bridges UI and business logic, allowing reuse of your core command implementations.

Benefits:

  • Clean separation of concerns
  • Improved testability
  • Flexible and extensible design
  • Undo/Redo support

Tradeoffs:

  • Additional abstraction layer
  • More classes and complexity
  • Overkill for small applications

value.text: Use the Command Pattern in complex applications where flexibility, extensibility, and undo functionality are important.

body.value:
The Command Pattern encapsulates actions as objects, enabling powerful features like undo/redo, decoupling, and extensibility. It is widely used in UI frameworks like WPF and in complex business applications.

When applied correctly, it leads to cleaner, more maintainable architecture — especially in systems with evolving requirements.
